DOC-407 | K-200 Battery Environmental Test Report

# K-200 Battery Environmental Test Report

Document number: MD-DOC-407-A. Revision: A. Type: risk-register. Author: Nina Schulz. Created: 2023-03-18. Last modified: 2024-01-30. Status: Released.

This document defines the requirements, interfaces, and verification approach for the Battery subsystem of the Kestrel K-200. Referenced parts: P-1085, P-1111, P-1028, P-1003.

citations.title: Replace propulsion motors P-1031 and ESCs P-1032 with P-1041/P-1042
citations.passage:
ECR-221 | Replace propulsion motors P-1031 and ESCs P-1032 with P-1041/P-1042

Engineering Change Request ECR-221.

Raised by: Marcus Thaine (Propulsion Lead), 2024-04-15.
Status: Approved. Approved by: Dr. Rhea Voss, 2024-04-22.

Summary: Replace all four instances of the TorqueDrive 4012 490KV motors (P-1031) and ControlAir 60A ESCs (P-1032) with TorqueDrive 4014 420KV Enhanced motors (P-1041) and ControlAir 80A Enhanced ESCs (P-1042).

Rationale: Two thermal events during April 2024 testing demonstrated that P-1031 motors cannot meet the 40°C ambient temperature operating requirement:
- KES-187 (2024-04-02): Fwd-port P-1031 motor reached 108°C winding temperature during 45-min endurance run at 30°C ambient, triggering thermal cutout. Winding temperature rating is 85°C maximum continuous.
- KES-188 (2024-04-08): Fwd-stbd P-1031 motor ESC (P-1032) repeatedly overcurrented due to thermal throttling, causing motor sync loss.

Root cause (established at WIKI-031 Propulsion Thermal Review, 2024-04-10): The 4012 490KV winding design has insufficient stator-to-case thermal conductivity. The P-1031 motor was selected in 2023 without a hot-day endurance test.

The TorqueDrive 4014 420KV Enhanced (P-1041) uses an improved winding geometry that reduces stator temperature by approximately 18°C at the same operating point (per TorqueDrive data sheet DS-4014E rev 2). Paired with the ControlAir 80A Enhanced ESC (P-1042, copper heatsink) and P-1051 heatsink, the thermal margin is restored to 22°C at 40°C ambient.

citations.title: Payload Gimbal Design Review — 2024-06-18
citations.passage:
WIKI-038 | Payload Gimbal Design Review — 2024-06-18

# Payload Gimbal Design Review — 2024-06-18

**Space:** K200  
**Meeting type:** Technical Design Review / Design Rejection  
**Attendees:** Cass Doyle (Payload), Felix Herrera (Integration), Aisha Mbeki (Test), Tobias Wren (Airframe), Sam Dreher (GNC)  
**Chair:** Felix Herrera  
**Minutes recorded by:** Felix Herrera

---

## Background

KES-134 (epic: payload gimbal integration) began with the original 2-axis gimbal sourced from GimbalTech Ltd in early 2024. During integration testing the gimbal exhibited structural resonance. KES-137 (bug: gimbal resonance during level flight) documented that the GimbalTech unit had a resonant frequency of 8.2Hz which coincided with the wing flutter mode of the airframe at 8–9Hz. At resonance the camera image was unusable — jitter exceeded ±4° peak-to-peak when the requirement in DOC-449 (Payload ICD) is ±0.5°.

## Root Cause

Cass Doyle presented the vibration analysis. The GimbalTech gimbal (no longer referenced as it is being rejected) had a resonant frequency of 8.2Hz due to the lightweight hollow-tube frame construction. The K-200 airframe wing flutter mode at cruise is 8–9Hz per the loads analysis (DOC-407). This was a design oversight — gimbal resonance was not considered during the original payload bay design.

Sam Dreher confirmed that active electronic stabilisation in SRC-021 (gimbal_driver.py) cannot compensate for structural resonance at the same frequency as the control bandwidth limit.

## Replacement Design

Cass Doyle proposed the redesigned gimbal assembly (P-1154, now Rev C) using hollow-shaft BLDC motors (P-1155 pitch, P-1156 roll, P-1157 yaw) from GB-Systems with a stiffer carbon fibre frame. The natural frequency of the new assembly is 24Hz, well above the airframe flutter mode.

ECR-221 | Replace propulsion motors P-1031 and ESCs P-1032 with P-1041/P-1042

Engineering Change Request ECR-221.

Raised by: Marcus Thaine (Propulsion Lead), 2024-04-15.
Status: Approved. Approved by: Dr. Rhea Voss, 2024-04-22.

Summary: Replace all four instances of the TorqueDrive 4012 490KV motors (P-1031) and ControlAir 60A ESCs (P-1032) with TorqueDrive 4014 420KV Enhanced motors (P-1041) and ControlAir 80A Enhanced ESCs (P-1042).

Rationale: Two thermal events during April 2024 testing demonstrated that P-1031 motors cannot meet the 40°C ambient temperature operating requirement:
- KES-187 (2024-04-02): Fwd-port P-1031 motor reached 108°C winding temperature during 45-min endurance run at 30°C ambient, triggering thermal cutout. Winding temperature rating is 85°C maximum continuous.
- KES-188 (2024-04-08): Fwd-stbd P-1031 motor ESC (P-1032) repeatedly overcurrented due to thermal throttling, causing motor sync loss.

Root cause (established at WIKI-031 Propulsion Thermal Review, 2024-04-10): The 4012 490KV winding design has insufficient stator-to-case thermal conductivity. The P-1031 motor was selected in 2023 without a hot-day endurance test.

The TorqueDrive 4014 420KV Enhanced (P-1041) uses an improved winding geometry that reduces stator temperature by approximately 18°C at the same operating point (per TorqueDrive data sheet DS-4014E rev 2). Paired with the ControlAir 80A Enhanced ESC (P-1042, copper heatsink) and P-1051 heatsink, the thermal margin is restored to 22°C at 40°C ambient.

WIKI-038 | Payload Gimbal Design Review — 2024-06-18

# Payload Gimbal Design Review — 2024-06-18

**Space:** K200  
**Meeting type:** Technical Design Review / Design Rejection  
**Attendees:** Cass Doyle (Payload), Felix Herrera (Integration), Aisha Mbeki (Test), Tobias Wren (Airframe), Sam Dreher (GNC)  
**Chair:** Felix Herrera  
**Minutes recorded by:** Felix Herrera

---

## Background

KES-134 (epic: payload gimbal integration) began with the original 2-axis gimbal sourced from GimbalTech Ltd in early 2024. During integration testing the gimbal exhibited structural resonance. KES-137 (bug: gimbal resonance during level flight) documented that the GimbalTech unit had a resonant frequency of 8.2Hz which coincided with the wing flutter mode of the airframe at 8–9Hz. At resonance the camera image was unusable — jitter exceeded ±4° peak-to-peak when the requirement in DOC-449 (Payload ICD) is ±0.5°.

## Root Cause

Cass Doyle presented the vibration analysis. The GimbalTech gimbal (no longer referenced as it is being rejected) had a resonant frequency of 8.2Hz due to the lightweight hollow-tube frame construction. The K-200 airframe wing flutter mode at cruise is 8–9Hz per the loads analysis (DOC-407). This was a design oversight — gimbal resonance was not considered during the original payload bay design.

Sam Dreher confirmed that active electronic stabilisation in SRC-021 (gimbal_driver.py) cannot compensate for structural resonance at the same frequency as the control bandwidth limit.

Cass Doyle proposed the redesigned gimbal assembly (P-1154, now Rev C) using hollow-shaft BLDC motors (P-1155 pitch, P-1156 roll, P-1157 yaw) from GB-Systems with a stiffer carbon fibre frame. The natural frequency of the new assembly is 24Hz, well above the airframe flutter mode.

sources.title: Configuration Baseline — K-200 System Architecture v1.0 (per DOC-401)
sources.body:
ECN-301 | Configuration Baseline — K-200 System Architecture v1.0 (per DOC-401)

Engineering Change Notice ECN-301. Programme configuration baseline.
Approved by: Dr. Rhea Voss, 2023-04-15.

This ECN formally establishes the K-200 System Architecture configuration baseline as defined in DOC-401 (System Architecture Document v1.0).

Key baseline parameters:
1. Autonomy level: SAE Level 3 conditional automation (per customer RFT-K200-002 requirement, recorded in WIKI-003 kick-off meeting and REV-631 review comments)
2. MTOW: 8kg
3. Endurance target: 60 minutes minimum at cruise
4. Battery chemistry baseline: LiPo (subject to thermal risk note in WIKI-003) — subsequently changed by ECN-312
5. Propulsion: quad-pusher, 4x motors (subsequently P-1031, changed by ECN-318 to P-1041)
6. Comms: 900MHz FHSS, 15km range (P-1125, subsequently upgraded by ECN-322 to P-1126 with encryption)
7. GNC: single-string FCU (P-1091), BVLOS open-water operations only
8. Redundancy: hardware fail-safe RTL if datalink lost >10s

This baseline is the reference against which all subsequent ECRs and ECNs are compared.

sources.title: Propulsion Thermal Review Meeting — 2024-04-10
sources.body:
WIKI-031 | Propulsion Thermal Review Meeting — 2024-04-10

# Propulsion Thermal Review Meeting — 2024-04-10

**Space:** K200  
**Meeting type:** Emergency Technical Review  
**Attendees:** Marcus Thaine (Propulsion Lead), Dr. Rhea Voss (CSE), Aisha Mbeki (Test), Tyler Moss (Firmware), Felix Herrera (Integration), Rowan Ellery (Electrical)  
**Chair:** Marcus Thaine  
**Minutes recorded by:** Felix Herrera

---

## Background

This emergency review was convened following two motor thermal events in the same week: KES-187 (fwd-port motor P-1031 thermal cutout during a 45-minute endurance run, 2 April 2024) and KES-188 (repeated ESC overcurrent on fwd-stbd motor P-1031, 8 April 2024). Both events occurred during warm-weather testing at ambient temperatures of 28–32°C. The original TorqueDrive 4012 490KV motors (P-1031) have shown a pattern of thermal issues that the thermal pads (P-1045) and standard heatsinks (P-1040 ESC mounting plate) are not adequately managing.

## Root Cause Summary

Marcus Thaine presented the thermal analysis. Key findings:

1. The P-1031 motor stator winding temperature was reaching 95–105°C during sustained hover at 28°C ambient, against a rating of 85°C maximum continuous.
2. The ESC (P-1032, ControlAir 60A) was throttling at 35°C case temperature due to its internal protection — cutting power to the motor and causing the observed overcurrent oscillation.
3. The root cause is insufficient thermal mass and poor stator-to-case conductivity in the 4012 490KV winding design. The motor was selected in 2023 without a hot-day endurance test, relying on the manufacturer's datasheet rating.

Tyler Moss confirmed the motor controller board (P-1044) thermal logging data showing temperatures up to 108°C at the motor windings.

## Options Considered

sources.title: K-200 Motor Qualification Test Report — P-1041/P-1042
sources.body:
DOC-445 | K-200 Motor Qualification Test Report — P-1041/P-1042

# K-200 Motor Qualification Test Report — P-1041 / P-1042

Document number: MD-DOC-445-A. Revision: A. Type: test-report. Author: Aisha Mbeki. Created: 2024-05-30. Last modified: 2024-06-10. Status: Released.

This report documents the qualification testing of the TorqueDrive 4014 420KV Enhanced motor (P-1041) and ControlAir 80A Enhanced ESC (P-1042) as replacements for P-1031 and P-1032. Change authority: ECN-318 / ECR-221. Background: thermal events KES-187 and KES-188, propulsion thermal review WIKI-031.

## §3 Thermal Test

Test configuration: P-1041 motor on P-1012 motor mount, P-1042 ESC with P-1051 heatsink, P-1044 motor controller board. Ambient temperature: 40°C (requirement maximum). Duration: 90 minutes at 75% throttle (representative endurance cruise).

Results:
- Motor winding temperature peak: 79°C (rated maximum: 85°C continuous). Margin: 6°C. PASS.
- ESC case temperature peak: 31°C (rated maximum: 70°C). Margin: 39°C. PASS.
- No thermal cutout events during 90-minute test. PASS.

Comparison with P-1031 at 40°C ambient (extrapolated from 30°C test data): estimated winding temperature would be 120°C — 35°C above rated maximum. FAIL expected.

## §4 Endurance Test

Duration: 3 x 90-minute flights at 40°C ambient. All flights completed without motor or ESC anomalies. Motor connector (P-1059) showed no heating. PASS.

## §5 Conclusion

P-1041 and P-1042 qualify for operational use per ECN-318 at ambient temperatures up to 40°C. P-1031 and P-1032 are obsolete and shall not be used in operational aircraft.
